entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Nikith Nagbushan Urs
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

My name is Nikith Nagbushan Urs, and I’m currently a third-year B.E. student in Computer Science Engineering (Data Science) at New Horizon College of Engineering, Bengaluru.

Alongside my academic journey, I currently serve as the President of the Innovation Club, where I work closely with teams of students to build innovative projects, organize technical events, and encourage creative problem-solving within the campus.

My interests lie in application development, innovation, and building platforms that make systems more efficient and accessible. One of the projects I worked on was the Central NHCE Application, which aims to centralize campus information and improve communication between faculty and students.

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as part of the "Hello SAP community"  initiative.

What was the most fun project you ever participated in and why?

One of the most exciting projects I worked on was developing the Central NHCE Application for my campus.

The goal of the application was to create a centralized platform where students can easily access important campus information, announcements, and updates. One of the major features of the application is department-specific announcements, which helps bridge the communication gap between faculty and students.

The application also focuses on a clean and user-friendly interface, allowing students to quickly navigate and find relevant information.

Working on this project was extremely rewarding because it allowed me to build something that can directly benefit students across the campus while making communication more structured and professional.

What do you imagine or predict is the future of AI?

I believe that AI will become one of the most powerful tools humanity can use.

While AI may automate many processes and make tasks easier, I strongly believe that core human emotions, creativity, and decision-making will always remain uniquely human.

AI should not be viewed as a threat but rather as a powerful tool and companion that enhances human capabilities. For people who learn how to work with AI and understand its potential, it will open many new opportunities.

In the future, I believe the world will move toward collaboration between humans and AI, where technology helps us solve problems faster and more efficiently.

What is your hobby?

One of my favorite hobbies is bike riding.

Bike rides give me the opportunity to relax and clear my mind after long hours of studying or working on projects. Riding allows me to explore new places, enjoy the journey, and take a break from screens and technology.

Sometimes, the best ideas for projects and innovations come to me while I’m out on a ride.

What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

One of the most important lessons my parents have taught me is to always respect elders and treat everyone with kindness.

No matter what achievements we accomplish in life, values such as respect, humility, and kindness define our character. These principles guide the way I interact with people in both my personal life and professional journey.

And now let’s hear from you!

What do you think will be the biggest impact of AI on the future of technology and society?

I would love to hear your thoughts in the comments!

entry.title: Hello SAP community! My name is Mohammed Asif and i'm excited to start my journey.
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community

My name is Mohammed Asif, and I’m currently pursuing Mechanical Engineering at REVA University in Bangalore, India.

I am very happy to be part of the Hello SAP Community initiative. This opportunity allows beginners like me to explore the SAP ecosystem and connect with professionals around the world.

As a student, I am interested in learning new technologies and understanding how SAP solutions help businesses manage their operations efficiently. Through the SAP Community, I hope to learn from experts, discover new ideas, and grow my professional network.

I look forward to sharing my learning journey and contributing to the  SAP  Community in the future.

Thank you SAP Community for this wonderful opportunity!

Here are the questions I chose to answer as a part of the initiative:

1.what is your professional life dream ?

My professional dream is to build a successful career in technology and engineering. I want to continuously learn new skills and work on innovative solutions that help organizations improve their efficiency. I also aim to contribute to the tech community and grow as a professional in the global technology ecosystem.

2.where do you see yourself in 5 years ?

In the next five years, I see myself as a skilled professional working in the technology industry. I hope to gain strong knowledge, practical experience, and work on real-world projects. I also want to keep learning and improving myself while contributing positively to my organization and community.

3.what is your hobby ?

One of my hobbies is learning about new technologies and exploring new ideas related to engineering and innovation. I also enjoy reading, learning new skills online, and improving my knowledge through different educational platforms.

4. which 4 thing do you absolutely want to achives in life ?

There are several goals I want to achieve in my life:

  • Build a successful professional career.
  • Continuously learn and develop new skills.
  • Contribute to society through knowledge and innovation.
  • Inspire others through my journey and experiences.

5.what is the best lesson your parents taught you ?

The best lesson my parents taught me is the importance of hard work, honesty, and patience. They always encouraged me to stay focused on my goals and never give up during difficult situations. These values continue to guide me in my education and future career.

 

My SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025 

 

 

My Experience at SAP TechEd Bangalore

Recently, I had the opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore, and it was an amazing learning experience for me. As a student interested in technology and innovation, being part of such a global tech event was truly inspiring. One of the most exciting parts of the event was exploring the Demo Pods, where SAP experts explained different technologies and solutions. It was great to see how SAP technologies like BTP are used to solve real-world business problems.

Key Takeaways & Highlights
While there was a lot to take in, these three themes defined the event for me:

The Power of Generative AI: The evolution of SAP Joule was front and center. It’s clear that AI is no longer a "plus-one" feature but the core engine driving developer productivity and business process automation.

A "Clean Core" is Non-Negotiable: There was a significant focus on the Clean Core Strategy. Learning about new extensibility patterns reinforced that staying close to the standard is the only way to ensure long-term agility and easier upgrades.

Driving Sustainability with Data: I was particularly inspired by the sessions on ESG reporting. Seeing how we can leverage BTP to turn sustainability goals into measurable data points is a game-changer for the modern enterprise.

 

 

 

That's a great photo! Capturing moments with a  community members like that adds a lot of authenticity

The Best Part of SAP TechEd:

The Community  While the product announcements and technical roadmaps are what bring us to SAP TechEd Bangalore, it’s the people who make the experience unforgettable. One of my personal highlights was the chance to connect directly with fellow community members and SAP experts on the show floor. Whether we were discussing the nuances of BTP at the Demo Pods or simply sharing our learning journeys, these interactions are where the real "tech magic" happens.

SAP TechEd Bangalore – A Great Learning Experience with My College Team:


I had the wonderful opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore along with friends from my college. It was a great experience for all of us to be part of such a global technology event. Our college group explored different sessions, demo pods, and interacted with SAP experts. We also had the chance to connect with members of the SAP Community and learn about new technologies like SAP BTP, AI, and cloud innovations.

Now, I would like to finish the post by asking you all a question which you're invited to answer in the comments section:

Which SAP modules offer the best career growth for beginners?

 

 

 

 

 

#HelloSAPcommunity #SAPcommunity #SAPLearning #SAPForBeginners #TechCommunity #FutureWithSAP

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Adithya Ajith and I’m excited to begin my journey here
entry.content:
My name is Adithya Ajith and I’m a student at Christ University , Bengaluru , India.

I am currently in the third semester of my MSc in Data Science, and I completed my bachelor’s in Computer Applications. I enjoy exploring the intricacies of data and working with datasets by applying different models to identify meaningful insights and outcomes.

I am delighted to be a part of the Hello SAP Community initiative and look forward to learning, sharing ideas, and connecting with people in the SAP ecosystem.

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as part of the initiative.

What are my hobbies? 

One of my favorite hobbies is reading, especially psychological thrillers with the suspenseful plots and complex characters. I also enjoy emotionally powerful stories, particularly the works of Khaled Hosseini . His writing captures human emotions and relationships.

Roaming around college with a book in hand

My reading list is quite long, and I’m currently looking forward to reading When the Lemon Trees Grow and The Palace of Illusions. If you’ve read either of these, I’d love to hear your thoughts!

From a recent trek

 

Along with reading, I enjoy exploring new places and hope to travel more in the coming years as part of my bucket list. I  like watching Urdu dramas for the poetic beauty of the language. I also enjoy learning new languages and regularly use Duolingo to practice German.

What is the best lesson my parents taught me? 

My parents taught me to respect every individual who crosses my path, regardless of their designation, age, gender, or any other external or material characteristics. 

This  has helped shape the way I view the world and further encouraged me to understand people beyond surface-level differences , I believe I have grown into a more considerate and empathetic person, both in my personal life and in my interactions with others.

What is my personal life dream?  

My biggest personal dream is to take my parents on trips across the country and around the world, especially my mother, who truly loves to travel. Being able to give them those experiences would mean a lot to me, as they have always supported me throughout my journey.

Another dream close to my heart is to provide meals once every month, to an old age home and an orphanage ,meals that they may not normally have access to and that are often considered a luxury. 

What was the most fun project I ever participated in and why?    

One of the most meaningful and enjoyable projects I participated in was during my bachelor’s when I was heading the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) initiative. The project focused on making simple beds for people living on roadsides, especially for children who often sleep on hard surfaces.

As part of the project, we visited different cloth manufacturing companies to collect fabric scraps and waste cloth. The plan was to connect and braid these pieces together to create durable beds. The initiative involved a lot of teamwork, thoughtful discussions, and brainstorming sessions, making it a very memorable experience.

What do I imagine or predict is the future of AI? 

No Traffic Signals, AI Footwear, and No More Manual Home Cleaning !

I predict a future where AI becomes a natural part of our everyday lives. Vehicles fully guided by AI, using satellite data and sensors to detect other vehicles and automatically choosing the best routes, making traffic signals unnecessary and reducing congestion. AI integrated footwear, where shoes automatically detect the size and arch of a person’s feet and adjust for the perfect fit. In homes, AI-based dust detection systems with centralized vacuum mechanism  automatically clean dust as soon as it accumulates, eliminating the need for daily sweeping ,wiping and preventing the asthma related problems.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore experience

Attending SAP TechEd @ Bangalore this year, was truly an incredible experience. It felt great to meet senior professionals from SAP and see how humble and approachable they were while interacting with us. I also had the chance to interact with people from different companies, learn about the opportunities available in the SAP ecosystem, and understand how organizations are integrating new technologies into their products and workplaces.

During the event, I also appeared for the Generative AI certification at the center. Even when there were some technical glitches, the team remained extremely calm and supportive, which was very reassuring. Another thing that left a strong impression on me was the confidence and clarity of the speakers throughout the event.

When I met Joule

The event also gave me the opportunity to expand my network, learn directly from industry experts, and gain a clearer perspective on the future of enterprise technology. 

At the SAP TechEd EVent @ Bangalore

I was also introduced to the SAP Community, a platform where people share ideas not only about technology but also about their hobbies, interests, and experiences. It was Moshe Naveh who formally introduced a group of us college students to the community and encouraged us to start contributing. This encouragement motivated me to write my first SAP Community blog, where I shared my reflections on emerging technologies and their impact on society. If you’re interested, feel free to check out my first blog here : Emerging Technology and Caged Thoughts 

Main highlights and SAP TechEd key takeaways 

One of the main highlights of SAP TechEd was learning how SAP is rapidly advancing  to build intelligent enterprise applications. The demo sessions showcased how AI and data are shaping the future of enterprise technology.

Another key takeaway was from the hands-on technical sessions, where we explored tools such as Knowledge Graphs in HANA Cloud, ABAP Cloud, UI5 development, AI agents, Cloud SDK, and MCP servers with CAP applications. The Generative AI deep-dive sessions on prompt optimization, benchmarking, and model evaluation were especially insightful and helped me understand practical approaches to building AI-driven workflows.

Overall, the event highlighted how AI, cloud platforms, and modern data architectures are transforming the way enterprises build and scale applications.

And now let’s hear from you! 😎

I would love to hear your thoughts as well.

What do you imagine or predict is the future of your topic (AI, Cloud, or any technology you are passionate about)?🤔

Share your ideas in the comments below. I’m really excited to read your perspectives and predictions. Also let me know what you think about my ideas!😊

entry.title: Re: Tax rate not calculating for intergration with vertex...
entry.content: I have checked the above conditions, and all the configurations you mentioned appear to be in place. I have also performed additional testing with different materials and analyzed the results. Based on my analysis, I observed that tax calculation is working correctly for materials with "numeric values" (for example, 69, 699, etc.). However, for "alphanumeric materials" (for example, TEST TAX, V4G8, etc.), the tax calculation is returned as "0%". Therefore, the conclusion is that "Vertex tax calculation is not working for alphanumeric materials and is returning 0%. While the issue has been identified, I am currently unsure how to resolve it. Can u please help on this?????

entry.title: Running Hugging Face AI Models with SAPUI5 and CAPM
entry.content:
Hi everyone 👋

After spending several hours researching and experimenting, I finally found a simple way to answer a question that many SAP developers have started asking:

“Is it possible to run AI models inside SAP applications using SAPUI5 and CAPM?”

The short answer is:

   Yes, absolutely!

In this blog, I will share how we can integrate Hugging Face AI models with SAPUI5 and CAPM, and how we can build a simple AI-powered application that can generate text responses and even images.

 

                                                                        Fig 1.1 Hugging Face Models sections

Now the Interesting Part: Can SAPUI5 Use AI?

        When I started this experiment, my first question was:

Can SAPUI5 directly call an AI model?

        The answer is yes, especially for text generation models.

To make this work, we only need three things:

- A Hugging Face account
-  An HF API Token
-  A model endpoint

Once you log in to Hugging Face, you can generate an HF Token from your account settings.

Then you can choose any model depending on your use case. Hugging Face provides model benchmarks and descriptions, so you can easily decide which one is best for:

  • Education
  • Coding
  • Reasoning
  • Chat
  • Content generation

Connecting Hugging Face with SAPUI5

Now let's talk about the implementation.

In the SAPUI5 controller, we can send a request to the Hugging Face API using JavaScript fetch().

The UI simply takes a user prompt, sends it to the AI model, and displays the response.

Example flow:

User enters a prompt:

       What is Hugging Face and SAPUI5?

SAPUI5 sends the prompt to the Hugging Face model.

The model generates a response and returns it.

Then SAPUI5 displays the result directly on the UI.

This makes it very easy to build AI chat assistants inside SAP applications.

 

                                                                                    Fig 1.2 Output- Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

Sample Code:

              Fig 1.3 sapui5 Controller.js - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

                      Fig 1.3 sapui5 view.xml - Hugging Face Ai Chat

But What About Image Generation?

      This is where things become interesting.

      When I tried running image generation models directly in SAPUI5, I realized something important.

      SAPUI5 works best with text-based AI APIs.

      For image generation models, we need a backend service.

This is where CAPM (Cloud Application Programming Model) becomes very useful.

 

Using CAPM for Image Generation

To generate images, we can integrate fal.ai, which provides inference APIs for advanced image generation models.

Some examples include:

  • black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev
  • Tongyi-MAI / Z-Image-Turbo

These models can generate high-quality AI images from text prompts.

For example:

"Portrait of a futuristic astronaut with glowing blue eyes"

The system sends this prompt to the AI model, and the model generates an image.

 

How CAPM Handles This

Inside the CAPM Node.js service, we create a custom action such as:

     generateImage

This action receives the prompt from SAPUI5.

Then CAPM calls the fal.ai API with that prompt.

The AI model generates the image and returns a URL of the generated image, which we send back to the UI.

This makes CAPM act as a bridge between SAPUI5 and AI models.

 

Why Use fal.ai Instead of Hugging Face Directly?

You might ask:

If we already use Hugging Face, why do we need fal.ai?

   Good question.

Some models listed on Hugging Face are actually hosted through different inference providers, including fal.ai.

For example:

"black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev"

This model is often executed through fal.ai inference APIs.

So fal.ai becomes an easy way to run those models with strong GPU performance.

Sample Code:

                   Fig 1.4 Output – CAPM - Hugging face Image generation

 

 

                  Fig 1.5 service.js - first half- Hugging face Image generation

 

                  Fig 1.6 service.js - second half - Hugging face Image generation

 

               Fig 1.7 Fal.ai Api key Generation - Hugging face Image generation

 

Final Result

After connecting everything together, we get a simple AI application:

The application can:

✅Generate AI text responses using SAPUI5
✅ Generate AI images using CAPM services
✅ Use Hugging Face models
✅ Run without training your own model

All of this can be done using free developer APIs, unless you need large-scale GPU workloads.

 

Conclusion

From this experiment, we can see that SAP applications can easily integrate with modern AI platforms.

The architecture becomes very simple:

                                         SAPUI5
                                              ⬇
                                  CAPM Service
                                             ⬇
                    Hugging Face / fal.ai AI Models

 

Using this approach, developers can build powerful applications such as:

  • AI chat assistants inside SAP
  • AI-powered knowledge systems
  • Image generation tools
  • Smart enterprise automation

And the best part is that you can even deploy your own AI model on Hugging Face and use it inside SAP applications.

sa

⭐ I hope this small experiment helps other developers who are curious about AI + SAP integration.

Happy coding! 🚀

entry.title: Modernizing integrations with event‑driven architecture and real customer use cases for SAP AEM
entry.content:
Customers want real-time business.  But many integration landscapes still rely on rigid, point-to-point connections that slow everything down.

So what’s the alternative?  Event-driven architecture.

In this upcoming session, we’ll explore how SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh (AEM) enables applications to communicate through events, creating flexible, scalable connections across SAP and non-SAP systems.

And we won’t stay theoretical.  You’ll see real customer scenarios showing how AEM helps distribute data instantly, reduce integration dependencies, and keep processes running smoothly across complex landscapes.

Plus, we’ll share practical guidance for identifying where event-driven approaches make sense in customer architectures, something partners are increasingly being asked about.

If you’re working with customers on modern integration strategies, this session is packed with insights you can immediately apply.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013314.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #EventDrivenArchitecture #ModernIntegration #SAPIntegrationSuite

entry.title: Re: Tax rate not calculating for intergration with vertex...
entry.content:
Good day @Thiru2203

Based on the payload comparison you provided, SAP is correctly sending both numeric and alphanumeric materials to Vertex. The issue appears to be that Vertex evaluates the alphanumeric product identifier differently and classifies it as non-taxable. Therefore, the most likely root cause is the product mapping or taxability rule issue for the material code sent in the payload on the Vertex-side, not a SAP limitation with alphanumeric material numbers.

Consider comparing the following in Vertex for one working and one failing material:

  • Product code received
  • Taxability driver field
  • Product mapping/ tax assist rule
  • Whether the rule expects exact match
  • Whether spaces or case matter.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Report RFBILA10 - Button "Data Sources for G/LAccount Balances" not working
entry.content:
Hello together,

we implemented archiving in our system via SAP DART/ILM. Now testing all applications on ACC System to make sure the reports can access the archived data. For Report RFBILA10 thats not the case.  Anyone else faced this problem? Thanks in advance for any hint/idea!

See the following for all details and things i already tryed.

System Infos

  • S4HANA ON PREMISE 2023 SP 02
  • Business Function "FIN_GL_CI_3" is activated

The Problem

The Button "Data Sources for G/LAccount Balances" not working in Report RFBILA10.

RFBILA10 bases on the logical database SDF, so the button functionality is programmed in report SAPDBSDF. This functionality is also called when using report RFBILA00 - there it works. The Problem in RFBILA10 is the if case in line 971.
As its complete standard and the variable get_events is not filled in the programm itself but earlier in some runtime generated programs im unable to find out on which condition it is set (to figure out customizing table or...)

What i tryed

  • Debugging to find out the root cause
    • things sadly to deep cause logical database technology
  • Debugging and change parameters so the button works
    • Didn't solved the problem - archiv data was not requested - but in the dialog you could see the archiv runs and select them
  • Playing around with ASDATASRC1 - Was not helping at all
    • https://me.sap.com/notes/596865
  • Check our archive settings (SARA/SARJ/SARI)
    • Checked the structure of the logical database SDF and looked up the corresponding archiv objects (FI_ACCOUNT, FI_DOCUMNT, FI_DOC_PPA,FI_TF_GLF) - via SARA i could see that the archvied data is correctly stored there

entry.title: Request for Flexibility/Evidence in PO Creation – "Create Purchase Order – Advanced" App
entry.content:
- When creating a Purchase Order (PO) using the "Create Purchase Order – Advanced" App, all types of materials are displayed during the material search, regardless of the Purchase Document Type. The client requires material selection to be restricted based on the Document Type (e.g., only Raw Materials should be displayed for Domestic Document Type).
- After selecting the Purchase Order Type, the client requires that only relevant fields be displayed in the Item Table. For instance, fields related to Purchase Requisition and Service should not be displayed for Domestic Purchase Orders.
- The client expects that clicking on an error message during PO creation should directly navigate the user to the exact field or section where the error exists, enabling quick identification and resolution.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Request for Flexibility/Evidence in PO Creation – "Cr...
entry.content:
Hi,

  1. The requirement to restrict material selection in the search help based on PO Document Type is not supported by standard functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 
  2. Based on the standard capabilities of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and the configuration described in SAP Note 3268822:

  • It is not possible to dynamically control the visibility of additional item-level fields based on Purchase Order Type.

  • The system only supports predefined field control options delivered by SAP.

3. Double click on the red dot which denotes the error and then the user will be directed to the field that has to be filled out correctly.

If you would like to request as a feature for 1st and 2nd point , we recommend submitting an idea through the SAP Customer Influence Portal, which is reviewed by Product Managers for potential inclusion in future releases. You can follow the steps in this KBA to submit your idea 

  1. Get started by opening SAP Customer Influence SAP Continuous Influence sessions for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition 
  2. Register and accept the Terms of Use
  3. Choose a session of your interest
  4. Create and submit your improvement request
  5. Take a look at existing improvement requests and start voting and commenting

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Automatically Remove Billing Blocks in Sales Order Billing Plans When Milestones Are Completed
entry.content:
1. Introduction

With the release of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, milestone billing for sales orders becomes smarter and more automated.
Traditionally, sales order billing plans created from project milestones require manual removal of billing blocks as each milestone is completed. The new feature eliminates this manual step: when the project manager enters the actual finish date for a milestone, the system automatically removes the corresponding billing block in the sales order billing plan.
This enhancement allows project financial controllers, project managers, and internal sales representatives to collaborate more smoothly and ensures billing can proceed promptly.

 

2. Step-by-Step Process Overview

Step 1: Create a Revenue Project with Planned WBS and Milestones

Using the Project Control (F3215) or Project Planning (F3822) app, the project financial controller creates a revenue project, defines WBS elements, and plans milestones. A project manager is assigned to take ownership of project execution.

For example, four milestones may be planned under four billing elements. Each milestone corresponds to a specific billing percentage. As milestones are completed, the billing plan should progress toward 100%.

 

Step 2: Release the Project

Once planning is complete, the project financial controller or project manager releases the project so that execution can begin.

Step 3: Create a Sales Order with Milestone-Based Billing Plan

In the Manage Sales Orders (F3893) app, the internal sales representative creates a sales order based on the released revenue project.
To enable milestone billing, the item category must be CBAO – Milestone-Billing Plan.

After entering the WBS element at the item level, the user can generate a billing plan automatically by selecting Create from Milestone.

At creation time, the billing plan is automatically blocked, and each billing plan line is tied to a specific milestone.
The billing-related indicator “Used for Billing” on the WBS element is also updated to “Yes”.

Step 4: Enter Actual Finish Date for Milestones

When a milestone is reached, the project manager updates the Actual Finish Date for that milestone.
Upon entering the date, the system informs the user via a message strip that the related billing block will be removed automatically after the project is saved.

Step 5: Automatic Removal of Billing Block

After the project manager saves the update, the system runs an asynchronous background process that removes the billing block from the corresponding billing plan item.
The sales representative will then see that the billing block is cleared — allowing billing to continue without manual intervention.
This automation reduces administrative overhead and ensures timely billing aligned with project progress.

3. Summary

This feature enhances the milestone billing process by:

  • Eliminating manual steps to remove billing blocks
  • Improving collaboration between project and sales teams
  • Reducing billing delays and administrative workload
  • Ensuring billing plans always reflect up-to-date project status

With SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, milestone billing becomes more automated, accurate, and efficient—supporting smoother end-to-end project-to-cash operations.

entry.title: Re: How to send dunning letters to customer Standard E-Ma...
entry.content:
Good day @Gebbi_22

Thank you for your follow up question. Please ensure you use the @ tag sign to ensure you get quicker follow-up responses.

To the point you raised, in OPD for Dunning, you should not enter a fixed email address if you want SAP to determine the recipient automatically. If you enter an email address directly in OPD, it overrides the default email address.

Your set up should be in Output Parameter Determination for Dunning in the Email Recipient determination step, leave the Email Address field blank.

With that setup, the system uses its standard recipient logic instead of a hard coded address. Note that by default, the recipient is the email address maintained in the Clerk Internet Address field, and only if you do not maintain that, does the default email logic come into play.

Hope this provides more clarity.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Moshe Naveh and I'm really excited about the new journey ahead of us
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

As you have probably read in my previous blog post about SAP TechEd 2025 and on Social media, this year’s SAP TechEd was unique and unforgettable in many ways. During the recent SAP TechEd season I had many special SAP Community moments: great conversations, made a lot of new community friends and often I was inspired by the people I met, each person in his or her unique topic and way. All those interactions started with “Hello! I’m Moshe from the SAP Community.” After after coming back from SAP TechEd Bangalore I thought how can we bring the same great energy that sparked during SAP TechEd to our SAP Community, so we can benefit from it year-round.

Today I’m very excited to announce a new SAP Community initiative, “Hello SAP Community”. The “Hello SAP Community” initiative will take us on an incredible journey to get to know the next generation of SAP professionals: what interests them, how they already shine in their career, their vision of the future, how they grow in the era of AI, their hobbies and much more!

When I got to meet SAP's next generation I was impressed by their creativity, energy, ambition and many other fascinating aspects! This sentiment only enhanced after I was fortunate to collaborate with our initiative’s first participants on their blog posts. I’ve learned so much in the process and I look forward to learning more from their future engagement in the SAP Community. This initiative, like the entire the SAP Community, regardless of how experienced you are in the SAP world, will enable us all to keep growing together!. 

So how will “Hello SAP Community” initiative run?

As we are targeting people who are new to the SAP world, we decided to host this initiative in the Beginners’ corner group. Our pilot of the initiative will start by featuring awesome students from Bangalore area . I was very fortunate to meet many of them in SAP TechEd Bangalore. Every week the participants will post introduction blog posts about themselves that will allow us to get to know them, learn from them and connect. They will answer preset questions and every participant will also add their own unique touch, insights and experiences. The SAP Community will gain fresh insights, interesting stories and a unique glimpse into the journey of becoming an SAP professional in the AI era, making it fascinating more than ever! The participants will gain the opportunity to share with the world the great things they are doing, their future plans, get a great advise from experienced SAP professionals and network. 

In order to be updated when new great blog posts are published, please visit the dedicated “Hello SAP Community” user tag and subscribe to it:

If you are new to the SAP world and would like to join the initiative and share about your journey, please send us an Email community@sap.com and we will send you further details about joining the initiative:).

Here are the preset questions that the participants were invited to chose from:

  • What is your professional life dream?  
  • Where do you see yourself in 5 years?  
  • How do you think the way you were raised affects your Career?  
  • What is your hobby?
  • What is a fun fact/story about yourself that people don't know?
  • Which 5 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life?  
  • What is the best lesson your parents taught you?  
  • If you could be a superhero (or are in your spare time) who would you be? What do you enjoy most in your work/studies and why?  
  • What is your personal life dream?  
  • What do you imagine or predict is the future of your topic? (AI, Cloud etc., be creative be daring, be entertaining)  
  • What was the most fun project you ever participated in and why? 

I invite you all to either answer one of these questions in the comments section or simply say Hello SAP Community:) 

I’m looking forward to another great SAP Community journey that started with a simple hello!:) I already encourage you all to read the amazing posts that were published!:)).

entry.title: Deep Dive: Customizing Print Forms in SAP Logistics Management
entry.content:
I recently wrote a blog about  Managing Print Forms in SAP Logistics Management, where I introduced the process of creating your own print forms in SAP Logistics Management (LGM henceforth). Whilst in that article I summarily touched upon Adobe Livecycle Designer, the tool you must mandatorily use to craft your own print templates, I had no intention of coming back to this topic until I received a couple of messages asking me to go deeper. So here it goes.

In LGM, print forms play a crucial role in logistics operations, particularly for shipping labels that accompany parcels and shipments to customers. While SAP provides standard out-of-the-box templates, business requirements often demand customization. This guide walks you through customizing a shipping label using Adobe LiveCycle Designer, covering three key enhancements:

  1. Adding a composite 'dimensions' field combining height, width, length, and unit of measure
  2. Creating a QR code pointing to a dynamically generated URL that includes the consignment number
  3. Adding a static image (e.g., a forklift icon to denote handling instructions)

These enhancements are represented below in the 'before / after' rendition of the Handling Unit Shipping Label we will utilise in this blog (left hand side: Standard out of the box SAP label | right hand side: Our customised label).

You can download the end result xdp file here, if you wish to inspect the customised form more closely yourself in Adobe Livecycle Designer.

I am also, if you prefer to watch the process rather than read about it, referencing here a video of the entire process from start to finish and the timestamps if you wish to go to a specific section:

  • 00:00 Introduction
  • 02:35 The print architecture in LGM
  • 05:50 Generating an out of the box shipping label form
  • 08:05 Moving to Adobe Livecycle Designer
  • 08:50 Introduction to the Adobe Livecycle Window
  • 17:00 Adding a composite 'Dimensions' Field
  • 32:30 Adding a QR Code
  • 42:40 Adding a static image
  • 46:20 Uploading and testing the customized form in LGM
  • 49:05 Testing the QR code

Before I go on to delve into the technical details, I want to briefly recap on the Print template architecture in LGM.

Print Template Rule Set

The first step in LGM, is to leverage a processing variant and using the print template ruleset (for warehouse), help LGM to determine which print form it should use to print a label when certain conditions are met. Indeed this is not a one size fits all conundrum! LGM is very flexible and easy to configure and uses a decision table to determine which label print form to use at runtime.

In our scenario, we have imagined that the label format was imposed on us by the carrier. As such our condition table will based on the criteria of:

  • Assigned carrier (LGMPRECAR1)
  • Document type (HUSL - Handling Unit Shipping Label)
  • LGM warehouse (L10) from witch the goods are shipped
  • --> Determine the print template ZPRE_HU_SHIPPING_LABEL_EN

Manage Print Templates

Each print template references an .xdp file (XML Data Package) that defines the form layout and data bindings. This file will be edited in Adobe LiveCycle Designer and must be uploaded back to SAP LGM after customization.

Downloading the Form Template

  1. Navigate to your print template in SAP LGM (via the Manage Print Templates app)
  2. Click on the .xdp file to download it to your local drive
  3. Save it to a dedicated working directory
  4. You can use the preview function (available since March 2026 update) in LGM to verify you're working with the correct template

Customizing the Form in Adobe Livecycle Designer

Open the form in Adobe LiveCycle Designer

  1. Launch Adobe LiveCycle Designer
  2. Open the downloaded .xdp file
  3. Immediately save it with a new name (e.g., Z_THE_NEW_NAME_OF_THE_FILE.xdp) to preserve the original (in case you need to start fresh!)

Understanding the Interface

You may need to customize the layout of the various panes that I am showing below, but I recommend you try to set it up in a similar manner, so as to show:

  1. A graphical representation of the form layout. There is no data, but gives you a very good idea of what the end product will look like.
  2. The 'Hierarchy' tab shows you the list of the fields, objects and sub-forms that are already included in your layout.
  3. The 'Data View' tab shows you the fields that are served by an Odata to the form template.
  4. The 'Scripting' pane will allow you to use 'FormCalc' or 'Javascript' to perform code aided changes or validations in your form
  5. The objects palettes are there to show the objects that you can add to your form 
  6. This section will show you various tabs that allow you to customise the formatting of a given field or object, as well as bind a form field to an Odata field.

 Data Binding : Connecting an Odata to your form

I have above already made references to 'Odata' and 'Data Binding'. LGM will utilise an Odata service at runtime, to serve data (weight, consignment number, delivery address, etc...) to your form. So when you add a field to your form (or want to see to what Odata field a form field is bound to), you will need to 'bind' it to the Odata Service.  You do this in the 'Binding' tab. This is illustrated in the image below:

  1. The form field 'Consigment'
  2. Is via the 'Binding' tab
  3. Connected to the 'Odata-field' Consignment (5)
  4. Served by the 'Odata-service' HandlingUnit

The Data View tab displays all available OData fields, including those not currently used in your form. This is essential when adding new fields to your template.

Customization 1: Adding a Composite 'Dimensions' Field

The Challenge

Instead of displaying four separate fields (height, width, length, unit of measure), we want a single field displaying:

120 x 80 x 60 CM

Implementation Steps

Step 1: Add the Required OData Fields

 

From the Data View tab, drag and drop these fields into your form:

  • Height
  • Width
  • Length
  • Unit of Measure Code

Step 2: Configure Field Properties

For each new field:

  • Set Appearance to "None" (removes the input box styling)
  • Set Caption Position to "Top" (in Layout tab)
  • Verify the Binding is correct (should auto-populate when dragging from Data View)

Step 3: Hide the Source Fields

Since these fields are only needed for calculation:

  • Select each field in the Hierarchy
  • Change Presence to "Hidden (Exclude from Layout)"

Step 4: Create the Display Field (for the Dimensions)

  • Duplicate an existing styled field (e.g., Gross Weight) to maintain consistent formatting
  • Rename it to txtDimensions 
  • Remove/check any existing data binding

Step 5: Add the Calculation Script

  • Select the txtDimensions field just created
  • Open the Script Editor pane
  • Select the Calculate event type and add the following script:
if (!handlingUnit.bdyMain.frmInfoBlockRow_1.frmLeftInfoBlock.height) {
	this.rawValue = "-"
} else {
	this.rawValue = height.rawValue  + " X " + 
			width.rawValue   + " X " +
			length.rawValue  + "  " + 
			unitOfMeasureCode.rawValue					
}

How This Works:

  • The calculate event triggers automatically when the form loads 
  • The script checks if height data exists - If yes, it concatenates all four values with proper formatting
  • If no, it displays a dash as a placeholder

Customization 2: Adding a Dynamic QR Code

The Business Case

Customers can upon receiving their package leave us a rating to express their (dis)satisfaction by scanning a QR code, but we want the QR code to be specific to a consigment. A dynamic QR code that leads to: http://www.fake-courrier.com/trackingid=CONSIGNMENT_NUMBER enables:

  • Customer feedback collection, specific to a consignment number
  • Tracking which shipment generated the feedback
  • Targeted customer service follow-up in case of a poor rating feedback

Implementation Steps

As we did in the previous step, we will use a field not bound to any Odata per se, to combine the fixed static URL of the web site and the consignment number that will be specific.

Step 1: Insert a new Text Field

  • From the object Library palette, select the object of type 'Text Field' and drag it onto your form layout
  • In the hierarchy tab, select your field and rename it (right click) to fullURLname
  • As before hide your field from the form
  • With your field selected, go to the scripting section and add a script of event type 'Calculate' like below to it
if (!handlingUnit.bdyMain.frmInfoBlockRow_1.frmLeftInfoBlock.txtConsignment) {
	this.rawValue = "-"
} else {
	this.rawValue = "http://www.fake-courrier.com/trackingid=" + txtConsignment.rawValue
}

Step 2: Insert a Barcode Object

  • From the Object Library palette, select Barcode type 'QR Code'
  • Drag it onto your form at the desired position
  • Resize to approximately 2cm x 2cm (minimum for reliable scanning)

 Step 3: Configure Barcode object

Connect the QR code barcode object to the above field we just created (that combines the fixed web site address + consignment number)

  • Select your QR code object
  • Go to the scripting section and add a event script of type layout:ready (Calculate is not available for this type of object)
  • Add the following code (it essentially says that 'this field' represented by '$' is equal to the value of the field 'fullURLname'
$ = fullURLname.rawValue

Customization 3: Adding a Static Image

Use Case

Display a forklift icon to indicate special handling requirements for heavy parcels.

Implementation Steps

Step 1: Insert an Image Field

  • From the Object Library, select the 'Image' field type
  • Drag it to the desired location on your form
  • Resize to appropriate dimensions (e.g., 3cm x 3cm)

 Step 2: Embed the Image

  • Select the Image Object in the form, then in the Object > Draw pallet
  • Browse to and import your image file (PNG or JPG recommended), by clicking on the 'folder' icon next to the URL field
  • Select your image in your chosen folder
  • Select the 'Embed Image Data' check box to ensure the image is part of the .xdp file

Step 3: Set Image Properties

  • Sizing: Set to "Scale Image Proportionally" to maintain aspect ratio
  • Resize and re-position your image as needed

Testing Your Customized Form

Once you are content with your changes, it is time to save your work, and upload your customized .xdp form back into LGM.

Step 1: Upload to SAP LGM

  • Save your .xdp file Adobe Livecycle Designer
  • Open the 'Manage Print Templates' app in LGM
  • Upload the new customized form, for your form template

Step 2: Create a Test Handling Unit

  • Open the Packing application 'Packing Work Center'
  • Create a handling unit with your choice of Packaging Material
  • Add item(s) to the handling unit and Set the dimensions infomration (e.g., 120cm height, 59kg weight)
  • Note down the Handling Unit number
  • Click Print Labels, to close the Handling Unit and print the label

Step 3: Verify Output

  • Go to the Monitor Handling Unit application
  • Enter your handling unit number
  • Click Open File (below the Shipping Label field) to view the generated PDF
  • Verify:
    • Dimensions is displayed correctly, showing heigh, width, length and unit of measure (e.g., "120 x 80 x 60 CM")
    • QR code scans properly and opens your browser to the correct URL and includes consignment number (use your phone or tablet for this)
    • Static image appears as expected

Conclusion

Customizing print forms in SAP Logistics Management empowers you to meet specific business requirements while maintaining integration with SAP's logistics processes with ease and flexibility. By understanding the architecture of print template rule sets, mastering data binding via OData, and leveraging Adobe LiveCycle Designer's scripting capabilities, you can create professional, functional shipping labels that enhance operational efficiency. The three customizations covered—composite 'dimensions' field, dynamic QR code, and static image—represent common real-world requirements. These techniques can be extended to many other scenarios.

Remember to always test thoroughly in a development environment before deploying custom forms to production, and maintain proper version control of your .xdp files.

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud, on AVC Need to derive BOM quantity...
entry.content:
Good day @Lord_Reaver

Please ensure you use the @ tag to ensure a quicker response.

Based on your description, the short answer to the points you raised is, the item category R and variable-size BOM are supported in Public Cloud but the ROMS1 variable-size dimension through the classic modelling pattern you mentioned is from on-prem or private cloud.

That would imply that your starter tenant is probably not the main reason. The exact mapping capability is just not handled that way in Public Cloud.

Question: Where exactly in Public Cloud is the mapping Characteristic → Variable Size Dimension maintained?

Answer: Public Cloud does not provide an AVC characteristic like Z_LONGITUD being bound to a variable-size dimension input. This is what you get in an on-prem environment.

Question: Is this done via Reference Characteristic, Dependency, or BOM dimension assignment?

Answer: for Public Cloud, this is not offered. As mentioned above, it relates to on-prem.

Question: Is this functionality fully available in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, or only in Private Cloud / On-Premise?

Answer:

  • Variable-size item itself is available in Public Cloud
  • The specific direct AVC characteristic-to-dimension mapping pattern is not something the I am aware of in Public Cloud
  • The classic reference-characteristic pattern is clearly an on-prem solution.

While your business requirement, Configured Length --> BOM component quantity --> correct MRP is valid, my recommendation is to consider raising a scope clarification or support incident with SAP.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Adding new item to an existing PO failing with I_PUCH...
entry.content:
That error message — “Numbering of child object does not match parent object” — is telling you that the CID references between the parent (PurchaseOrderItem) and child (AccountAssignment) are inconsistent. In RAP/behavior framework, every child entity you create must reference the same %cid that was used when creating its parent, otherwise the framework cannot link them correctly.

Having said that use the same CID for both parent and child linkage. Typically, you only need one %cid for the parent item. The child account assignment should reference that same %cid via %cid_ref. Don’t generate a second CID (lv_cid1) for the child — that’s what causes the numbering mismatch.

entry.title: Re: DP90 shows Original Amount but Open Amount = 0 in Res...
entry.content:
To troubleshoot your issue, follow the below steps

  • Verify DIP profile mapping for the relevant cost elements (the ones you see in CJI3).
  • Check the sales order item category and ensure it is billing relevant for RRB.
  • Run DP96 for the WBS element to reset the billing status, then retry DP90.
  • Confirm that the WBS element is correctly assigned in the sales order item and matches the costs in CJI3.
  • If still no open amount, simulate DP91 with the DIP profile to see if costs are picked up — this helps isolate whether the issue is DIP mapping or SD integration.

entry.title: SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 8: SAP Customer Experience: Mobile Daily Catch-Up for Sales
entry.content:
The new Daily Catch-Up within the SAP Sales Cloud mobile app is a real game changer for sales reps, since it directly helps you sell more, by helping you prioritize your day, with a three-step flow highlighting upcoming activities, missed items from the day before, and high-priority deals and leads that otherwise would fall through the cracks. Not only that: it is delightful to use, with nice animations and haptics.

This series of blog posts covers innovations in user experience in our public cloud products from the last six months or so. In case you missed it, Part 1 provides general updates about Joule, SAP Work Zone and SAP Mobile Start, along with links to all the other parts.

 

Revolutionizing the Sales Experience with Daily Catch-Up

Daily Catch-Up is a major user experience innovation designed specifically for mobile sales reps, and is another example of how we are making work form enjoyable, helping users achieve better outcomes with AI.

We’ve learnt from extensive user research and conversations with customers that sales reps often struggle to prioritize their day due to "scattered signals" that surface throughout the day across emails, chats, and various notification channels. The Daily Catch-Up addresses this "notification overload" by unifying these signals into one predictable, guided experience.

Figure 1 gives a first impression: the first two examples show how you can access the catch-up: via a notification, or from the daily summary card on the SAP Sales Cloud apps’s start page. The third example shows the catch-up screen.

Figure 1: Notification and Daily Summary on home screen lead you to your daily catch-up. Alt Text: Three images of mobile phones, as described in the text. The third example shows a card “Visit Kixo Plant” with date and time, and a “Why Prioritized?” section, statint it is starting in 2 hours, with a “Reschedule” and “AI Summary” button and “Mark as Complete”.

Enjoyable: Intuitive and Tactile Design

The new Daily Catch-Up reimagines the interaction model for mobile CRM by moving away from traditional, cluttered lists to a modern, card-based layout.

  • Reduced Cognitive Load: By showing only one card at a time, we keep your attention focused on what matters most without the distraction of competing items. Each card has a clear front and back. A simple tap reveals additional details only when the user needs them, keeping the primary view clean and focussed.
  • Tactile Design: Swiping through cards feels natural, quick, and thumb-friendly, with almost no learning curve. You can use directional swipes to instantly mark tasks as complete or save them for later, without extra taps or navigation.
  • Delightful Experience: Smooth animations and subtle haptics add a touch of delight and give the flow a light, game-like quality. The overall experience feels faster, more intuitive, and more enjoyable on a small mobile screen – as you can see in the animation in Figure 2 below.

Figure 2: Examples of the animations for flipping the card, and for swiping to the next one within a step. Alt Text: Video of a mobile phone, showing how the card rotates around a vertical axis when tapped on, and how you can swipe it upwards towards top right.

Better Outcomes: Heuristic Prioritization and “Top 5 Focus”

We are helping Sales Reps move beyond simple chronological tracking to achieve better business outcomes through smart prioritization.

  • Heuristic Scoring: This new version of Catch-Up uses a scoring system that combines machine learning inputs and local context to calculate a priority score. This ensures Reps see what is most urgent and impactful first.
  • The "Top 5 Focus": Each morning, the system surfaces a "Top 5 Focus" list of prioritized items across opportunities, leads, quotes, orders, accounts or cases that require immediate attention. Ranking follows the same principles of business impact and urgency. Each card includes a short, two-line guidance summary to provide instant clarity.

One Consolidated, Guided Flow

Catch-Up brings everything together, providing a single, predictable way for users to start their day. There are two ways for you to get started: from contextual notifications, or from the app’s homepage, as shown in Figure 3 below:

Figure 3: Contextual notifications and the Daily Summary card on the home page lead you to Daily Catch-Up. Alt Text: Two images of mobile phone. On the left, schematically three notifications are shown on the start screen: “Start Your Day, Focussed”; “You’re at San Francisco International Airport – Do you want to review today’s schedule or follow-ups while you wait?”; “You’re near multiple accounts/leads but don’t have a visit scheduled – Do you want to create a visit?”. On the right the start page, with a Daily Summary card for catch-up, stating “Nice work! You’ve kepe up your Catch-up streak for 3 days straight!”.

Catch-Up has a Three-Step Guided Flow:

  1. Upcoming activities: Highlighting what’s next on the calendar.
  2. Missed items: Covering anything that was overlooked the previous day.
  3. “Top 5 focus”: the high priority items, ranked as described above, that often fall through the cracks because they do not appear in the upcoming activity list.

Figure 4 below shows an animation of how users can move from step 2 to step 3 in the guided flow. You see that on the last card in step 2 you have action buttons to set the status or log a call. Once you swipe it away, you see an example of our new illustration design, with a message saying that CRM is now up-to-date. Clicking on Next Step brings you to step 3, with a prioritized deal. Each card has a “Why Prioritized?” section, explaining why the system has chosen to prioritize this item.

Figure 4: Moving from Step 2 to Step 3. Alt Text: Video of a mobile phone, showing the user swiping up the last card in step 2, getting a message that “Your CRM is now up-to-date – move to next step”, clicking on “Next step” brings up a message “Terry, here are the top 5 things for you to focus on today”, showing a card with the first one.

With Daily Catch-Up, the SAP Sales Cloud mobile app becomes a tool that really helps you sell more, rather than feeling like a tracking tool that your company wants you to use. It becomes a "habit builder" that helps you hit your quotas and stay focused on your real day-to-day goals.

Find out more in the documentation:

  • Mobile: Daily Catch-Up.

Get the SAP Sales Cloud mobile app in one of the app stores:

  • SAP Mobile Sales in Apple app store.
  • SAP Mobile Sales in Google Play.

 

Continued soon…

I hope you enjoyed this eigth part, covering SAP Customer Experience – SAP Sales Cloud.

I will post part nine next week, covering latest UX innovations for UI technologies.

Part 1 of the series lists all the parts, and links to those parts that have already been published:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

entry.title: Impact of Material Type in MM01 on SAP Configuration and Processes
entry.content:
Hello,

I would like to better understand the technical and functional impact of the Material Type defined in MM01.

From my understanding, material type controls several aspects of the material master and logistics processes, such as:

  • Number range assignment

  • Allowed views in the material master

  • Procurement type

  • Quantity/value updating

  • Account determination in FI

  • Field selection in material master

  • Integration with other modules

However, I would like to understand in more detail:

  1. Which SAP tables and configuration objects are directly influenced by the material type?

  2. How does the material type affect valuation, accounting, and stock management behavior?

  3. What is the relationship between material type and account determination (OBYC)?

  4. How does it influence procurement processes (purchasing vs production)?

  5. Are there any best practices for defining custom material types (Z material types) in SAP MM?

I would appreciate a deeper explanation of the technical and configuration impact of material type within SAP MM and its integration with FI and other logistics modules.

entry.title: Re: consumption group in mm02
entry.content: MM02 is a master data transaction and does not display or control movement types. If you want to analyze consumption by the new group, the right approach is to enhance reporting logic (e.g., CDS views or KPI dashboards) so that all relevant movement types are classified under the new group. You can optionally add a custom field in the material master to tag the material with the group, but the consumption itself will always be visible only in transactional reports, not in MM02

entry.title: SAP GROW – Service Entry Sheet does not recognize quantity
entry.content:
Hello

We kindly request SAP to:

  2. Clarify the recommended service model for fixed-price services that require service entry and approval
  3. Inform whether there is any:
    • SAP Note
    • KBA
    • Known product limitation
  4. Provide SAP best practice guidance to avoid this issue in future purchase orders

 Business Impact

This issue prevents the registration and approval of already executed services, directly impacting:

  • Procurement operations
  • Service approval process
  • Supplier invoicing and payments

 

 

 

Thanks


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAP GROW – Service Entry Sheet does not recognize qua...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your detailed analysis.

  1. Supportability of the Purchase Order Model
    The described purchase order model is supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and GROW with SAP when service procurement is created using service lines in the PO (Item Category K – Service). The Service Entry Sheet (SES) determines the value based on the quantity entered in SES and the pricing maintained in the PO service lines.

  2. Recommended Service Model for Fixed-Price Services
    SAP recommends using the Lean Service Procurement model, where:

  • Service lines are maintained in the Purchase Order

  • SES is created with actual quantities entered at the service line level

  • The system automatically calculates the value based on PO pricing.

  1. Relevant SAP Documentation
    You may refer to the following SAP Knowledge Base Articles:

  • KBA 3685262 – Service Entry Sheet cannot be entered by amount

  • KBA 3434955 – Net Order Price shows 0.00 in Service Entry Sheet

These articles explain that SES valuation is quantity-based and the amount is calculated automatically from the PO price.

  1. SAP Best Practice
    To avoid similar issues in future purchase orders, SAP recommends:

  • Maintaining service lines with quantity and price in the PO

  • Entering quantities at the service line level during SES creation

  • Ensuring price unit and quantity are aligned for correct calculation

Following this standard service procurement approach will ensure that SES values are correctly calculated and posted.

Kind regards.

SAP SUPPORT

entry.title: From Zero to “It Works!” — A Journey of installing Cline for Windows users
entry.content:
This document provides a detailed English step-by-step guide for installing the Cline extension in Visual Studio Code and configuring it to work with the Hyperspace LLM Proxy on Windows.

Hyperspace LLM Proxy = the secure gateway for the company’s internal AI services.
It allows your local tools (such as Claude Code or Cline) to access LLMs through the security proxy required by the company. The installation of hai CLI will achieve this.

Cline (VS Code extension) or Claude Code = your AI coding assistant.
However, Cline itself cannot directly access the company’s internal AI models. So we need the LLM proxy installed first.

Install the hai CLI for Windows

Connect Your Tools (Choose Either Cline or Claude Code)

For Cline installation

In general, you can follow the above link one by one to finish the installation.

Just in case the above guide is not detailed enough or you are not familiar with the command line behavior, please see steps below:

1.      Install the hai CLI (Command Line Interface) tools for Windows (Refer to Link)

 

The Complete Flow as preview:

Step 1: Download hai-windows.amd64.exe

        ↓

Step 2: Create new folder and Rename-Item (rename the file)

        ↓

Step 3: Move-Item

        ↓

Step 4: Add hai to your PATH (COMPLETE HERE ✓)

        ↓

Step 5: hai version (just verification, not installation)

 

Step details:

• Step 1: Download ` hai-windows.amd64.exe` from the hai-cli releases page.

 

Double click on the first highlighted version, it will be downloaded to your default location:

 

 

• Step 2: Create new folder and Rename file

  • Open Windows Powershell
  • Press Win + X and select "Windows PowerShell" or "Windows Terminal"
  • Or search for "PowerShell" in the Start menu
  • • Execute command 1 to create new folder: New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "C:\SAPDevelop\hai"

 

  • • Navigate to the download location by executing the following command in PowerShell and press enter:

cd C:\Users\I070363\Downloads

 

 

  • Execute command 2: Rename-Item hai-windows.amd64.exe hai.exe

 

To address the curious you might have, you will find the file name been renamed from ‘hai-windows.amd64.exe’ to ‘hai.exe’.

 

• Step 3: Move-Item

Execute command 3: Move-Item -Force hai.exe "C:\SAPDevelop\hai\hai.exe

This will place hai.exe into a folder such as C: \SAPDevelop\hai\hai.exe.

To be honest, if you have issues with the above, you can just create a folder C:\SAPDevelop\hai, and move your hai-windows.amd64.exe hai.exe to this folder, and rename it to hai.exe.

 

 

• Step 4: Add hai to your PATH (one-time setup).

Please navigate to the following section of this webpage and copy.

 

# Add hai directory to user PATH

$haiPath = "C:\SAPDevelop\hai"

$userPath = [Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "User")

if ($userPath -notlike "*$haiPath*") {

    [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "$userPath;$haiPath", "User")

}

# Refresh PATH for current session

$env:Path = [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "Machine") + ";" + [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "User")

 

 

Launch Powershell and paste those command all in once.

 


This is the actually final step of the installation.

• Step 5: Verify installation using: hai version.

 

If you receive the same message after ENTER. This tells that you have successfully installed Hai CLI.

2. Start the Hyperspace LLM Proxy

• Run in PowerShell: hai proxy start --port 6655

 

  • A GUI window appears showing Status: Ready, Proxy URL, and API Key.

 

  • Do NOT close this window — the API key is only valid while it is open.

Now we have hai CLI installed and running.

3. Install Visual Studio Code (VS Code)

Step 1: Open the official VS Code download page

Visit https://code.visualstudio.com and click ‘Download for Windows’

 

Step 2. Download the VS Code installer

Your browser will automatically download the installer. It will be saved by default to your Downloads folder:

C:\Users\I070363\Downloads

 

Step 3. Run the installer

Open your default download file, please proceed to double click on VSCodeUserSetup-x64-1.111.0.exe and follow the installation guide.

When the installation window appears: Check “I accept the agreement”. Then click Next.

Step 4. Choose the installation path (recommended to keep default)

The default installation path is usually:

C:\Users\I070363\AppData\Local\Programs\Microsoft VS Code

Keep the default → click Next.

Step 5. Select additional tasks

When you see “Select Additional Tasks”, check the following:

✔ Add "Open with Code" action to Windows Explorer file context menu

✔ Add "Open with Code" action to Windows Explorer directory context menu

✔ Add to PATH

Step 6. Click Install

The installation takes about 10–20 seconds. When it's completed, click Finish.

 

4. Install the Cline Extension in VS Code

• Open VS Code → Extensions panel.

Click the desktop icon: Visual Studio Code

or search for it: Press Win key → type “code” → Enter

 

 

• Search 'Cline' and install.

In the left sidebar, click the: Extensions icon (the square icon)

In the search bar on the right, type: Cline

You will see the extension:

 

Click Install.

The installation takes about 5 seconds.

A new icon appear on left bar and tick ‘Bring my own API key’ and click ‘Continue’.

 

5. Configure LiteLLM Provider and select models

  • Open the configuration panel by clicking ‘Settings’, make the following configuration accordingly

API Provider: Select LiteLLM

Base URL: Enter http://localhost:6655/litellm/v1

API Key: Paste the API key displayed in the proxy terminal from Step 1

In the Model field, type ‘anthropic--claude-sonnet-latest’

After all the above settings, click Done.

 

You're all set!

entry.title: Re: #CoE#TFAL Treatment of Opening Balances After Histori...
entry.content:
Good day @PDIEGO,

Your approach is valid and aligns with the standard mid-year go-live pattern for S/4HANA Public Cloud. Let me add a few clarifications that may help you and others reading this.

Your design is the intended approach, not a workaround.

If your cutover falls mid-FY2026 (period 6), then Historical Balance for pre-cutover periods + G/L Balance & Open Items at the cutover point is exactly how SAP designed the migration to work. This is the standard pattern for any go-live that doesn't fall on fiscal year start.

Clarification 1: Reversal happens at the migration key date, not at fiscal year-end.

Historical balances reverse at the date configured in the Define Settings for Legacy Data Transfer app (SSCUI FINS_MIG_CTRL_1), not automatically at year-end. In your case, this date should be the end of period 5/FY2026 (= end of September 2025 in your April–March calendar). Both FY2025 and FY2026 period 1–5 historical balances reverse at this single point. Misconfiguring this date breaks the entire design.

→ SAP KBA 2754615 – Migration Cockpit: Posting Date / Migration Key Date

Clarification 2: The G/L Balance load at cutover must carry the full cumulative balance.

Since all historical balances net to zero at the migration key date, the G/L Balance & Open Items load in period 6 must represent your complete balance sheet position as of cutover — not a delta or movement since the historical load. This is the single most common mistake in this scenario.

→ Legacy Data Transfer Options – SAP Help (latest/2602)

Clarification 3: Don't forget sub-ledger open items.

G/L Balance & Open Items alone won't give you a complete productive opening. You also need separate migration objects for:

  • FI – Accounts Receivable Open Item for customer receivables
  • FI – Accounts Payable Open Item for vendor payables
  • Fixed Asset (incl. balances and transactions) for asset sub-ledger

Without these, your sub-ledger detail won't match GL totals and downstream processes (dunning, payment runs, depreciation) won't work correctly.

→ Migration Objects for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (latest/2602)

Clarification 4: BCF vs. NPM — choose deliberately.

For the Historical Balance control parameter, Balance Carry Forward (BCF) gives cleaner cumulative period-level reporting in Display G/L Account Balances. Next Period Movement (NPM) works better for P&L period movements. For a full fiscal year like your FY2025, consider using both across separate migration projects if reporting clarity matters to your business.

→ SAP KBA 2943035 – Migration of Historical Balances → Historical Balances – SAP Help (latest/2602)

In summary: your approach is sound and standard for mid-year go-lives. Key things to double-check: (1) migration key date is set to the exact cutover point, (2) the GL Balance load carries the full cumulative position, and (3) sub-ledger open items are migrated with their dedicated migration objects.

For a good end-to-end overview of the migration process, see also this SAP Community blog: Historical Balances Migration in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

Best regards,

Marcela (SAP Core Finance team)

entry.title: Configuring Concurrent Login Control with SAP Cloud Identity Services
entry.content:
What Is Concurrent Login

Concurrent login refers to a situation where a single user account maintains multiple active sessions simultaneously, typically from different browsers, devices, or locations.

For example, a user might be logged in at the same time from:

  • Laptop browser

  • Mobile phone

  • Another workstation

Without restrictions, the system allows all sessions to remain active.

Example scenario:

  • User Account
    1. Browser Session (Laptop)
    2. Browser Session (Desktop)
    3. Mobile Session

Allowing unlimited concurrent sessions may introduce several risks:

  • Account sharing between multiple individuals

  • Security exposure if credentials are compromised

  • Difficulty enforcing session governance

  • Potential licensing misuse

To mitigate these risks, organizations often implement concurrent session restrictions through their identity provider.

 

Concurrent Login Control in SAP Cloud Identity Services

SAP Cloud Identity Services includes Identity Authentication Service (IAS), which manages authentication and session control for SAP cloud applications.

IAS allows administrators to configure concurrent access policies that control how many active sessions a user may have at the same time.

When a concurrent login attempt occurs, CIS can enforce different behaviors depending on the configuration.

Typical behaviors include:

ModeBehavior
AllowMultiple sessions are allowed
WarningUser is notified about an existing session and may terminate it
ErrorOnly one active session is permitted

This control is enforced during the authentication process before access is granted to the target application.

 

Configuring Concurrent Login in SAP Cloud Identity Services

Concurrent login behavior can be configured directly in the SAP Cloud Identity Services Administration Console.

Step 1 – Access the CIS Administration Console

Log in to the SAP Cloud Identity Services Admin Console with administrator privileges.

https://tenantID.accounts.ondemand.com/admin

 

Step 2 – Navigate to the Application

Go to Applications:

 

 
Select the application for which you want to control concurrent sessions. Example: SAP Ariba

 

Step 3 – Configure Concurrent Access

Within the application configuration, locate the Authentication and Access section.

 

The default selection is Allow as below:

 

Select the appropriate policy based on your organization's security requirements.

Check the configuration again:

 

Validation Example Using SAP Ariba

To verify the configuration, we can test the behavior using SAP Ariba, which is integrated with SAP Cloud Identity Services and Corporate Identity Provider Entra ID for authentication.

The authentication flow is as follows:

 
User -> SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) -> Entra ID -> SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) -> Ariba

CIS authenticates the user before granting access to Ariba.

Test Scenario (Warning)

Step 1 – First Login 

Open Browser Microsoft Edge and log in to SAP Ariba using the test user account.

 

Step 2 – Second Login Attempt

Open Browser Chrome(or another device) and attempt to log in with the same user account.

CIS detects that an active session already exists.

Step 3 – Concurrent Login Detection

The user will see a message as below. This message allows the user to terminate the previous session and continue.

Step 4 – Session Switching

If the user selects Sign out and continue, the system will:

  • Terminate the existing session

  • Allow the new session to proceed

The user will then be logged into SAP Ariba through the new session.

Step 5 – Go back to the old browser and login again, same message showing here.

If the user selects Stay Signed In, the system will not terminate the existing session and show below:

 

Test Scenario (Error)

Configuration change:

 

Step 1 – First Login 

Open Browser Chrome and log in to SAP Ariba using the test user account.

Step 2 – Second Login Attempt

Open Browser Microsoft Edge(or another device) and attempt to log in with the same user account.

CIS detects that an active session already exists.

Step 3 – Concurrent Login Detection

The user will see a message as below. Error shows and no login allowed:

 

Key Benefits of Concurrent Login Control

Using SAP Cloud Identity Services to enforce concurrent login policies provides several benefits.

Improved Security

Restricting concurrent sessions helps prevent credential sharing and unauthorized access.

Centralized Identity Governance

Policies configured in CIS apply consistently across multiple SAP cloud applications.

Better Session Management

Administrators gain better control over authentication sessions and user behavior.

Enhanced Compliance

Session restrictions help organizations meet internal security policies and regulatory requirements.

Useful Documentation

SAP Cloud Identity Services Overview: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services

Notes: 

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003327459

Configure Concurrent User Access to the Application:

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/configure-concurrent-user-access-to-application

Conclusion

Concurrent login control is an important security mechanism for managing user sessions and preventing account misuse.

With SAP Cloud Identity Services, administrators can configure and enforce concurrent session policies centrally for SAP cloud applications.

By configuring concurrent login policies and validating them through applications such as SAP Ariba, organizations can strengthen their identity security while maintaining a consistent user authentication experience.
